Hello. Good morning. Happy Thursday. So the next speaker that we're going to introduce today, or I'm going to be introducing, the royal way, is going to be introducing today is someone near and dear to my heart. I wanted to make this like a wrestling intro where it's like the master of disaster. But Layla is the single greatest thing that ever happened to me and also the single greatest thing that ever happened in the business. And I'm so lucky to have found her and to have swiped right on that one day, that fateful day. And there's a great story behind that, but I won't get into it. But she's an unbelievable human being in general. She's incredibly giving and generous of her time, herself, our money, everything. And she's really the leader of our company in, I would say, most ways. And so she's a wealth of knowledge. She constantly is reading books on the subject and educating herself. And the things that I told you yesterday about her being able to coach herself on a dime and being incredibly disciplined, it's just shown in how much she's grown this company. Because at the end of the day, I can market and sell whatever I want. But unless we can fulfill, it's short lived. And then eventually, and especially in a community like this one where everyone knows everyone, if we weren't delivering what we were promising, then two-thirds of you guys wouldn't be here. So she is, I mean, like, we're a $30 million company now. And we're, yeah. And it's all really thanks to her. And so I think in my opinion, I told Alex Charfin, he just walked in, I was like, I think this is going to be the best presentation of the event, in my opinion. And I'm speaking four of the other times. But I think that you guys will be able to get more out of this one than probably anything else. And this one will show you, step by step, the disciplines that you'll need to adopt and the ways that you'll need to see the frameworks that you need to be able to see your own employees through and how you really build the infrastructure, the backbone behind the amazing stuff that we can do on the front end. So if you guys wouldn't mind joining me in a round of applause and standing up for my amazing, beautiful, fantastic wife, Layla Hermozzi. What's up? So I actually didn't hear anything he said. But it sounded nice from the couple things I caught when you guys were going in and out of the door. So welcome to day two. And I will kind of jump right into it. So I practiced the speech a couple of times. And at one point it was like 45 minutes and then the other time it was like 90 minutes, the other time it was like an hour and then it was 45 again. So I don't know how long it will be. Probably depends on how fast I talk. But I really felt like because I don't get to talk to you guys a ton and I'm more behind the scenes, I wanted to give you as much value as I could in this presentation and in the things that are going to come to fruition from it in a couple of weeks for you in legacy. So that being said, I wanted to give acknowledgments to the people because honestly, like you guys know, it's really who you learn from. And it might be a surprise to some of you, but my husband is the first. Alex taught me more than anything about building a team is that trusting people is the number one. And the reason that he was able to teach me that is because he trusted me. And so he has trusted me to do more than I ever thought I could do. I had never built a company, run a company, managed a company or anything of that sort. And he just completely entrusted that to me. Alex Charfin, who I'm sure many of you met while you're here or a fanboy girl over who has, we met back in July when it was two of us and he was able to paint a picture of what we could create bigger than what we had in mind at that time and has been an incredibly great friend and mentor since then. Peter Drucker, Craig Gruchel, Henry Esposia, Vern Harnish, Simon Sinek, Patty McCord. A couple of those are other people that we paid for mentoring from and then a couple of them are people that I just read their books and follow them and stalk them, essentially. So I know a lot of you asked, like, what do you read and look into? I'm like, I don't know. I read what is applicable. But I think when it comes to, like, a lot of times I want to ask, like, why are you reading the book? What's the goal? And so sometimes it's more specific. Like, I YouTube things, I watch interviews. Oftentimes, watching how someone acts and just seeing them in their natural state, you're going to learn more than reading their book. So what I want to share with you today, part one is going to be identifying where you are right now. So kind of, like, on the weekends, my, like, fun thing to do is, like, survey the group, the gym legacy and, like, go through it because normally if I do it during the week I get, like, sucked in because it's so fun to watch all the interactions. And I kind of see where I believe most of you are right now based on if you just came into gym legacy or if you've been in here for a year. And then two is the internal five core function. So something that Al Charfin introduced us to was he has the concept of the five external core functions. And I kind of had this epiphany, maybe back in September. And I was like, there's totally, like, the internal as well. So we will be going over that majority of the time. And then what I want you guys to get from the speech. So, like, when I go about writing a speech, I'm like, what is it going to be about? What do I want them to get from it so that, like, each time when I'm going through writing this I know exactly, like, I can reflect back on something, right? It's like, what's the goal of this? And it is that you can walk out believing that you can build and lead a great team and you have the clarity and the motivation to do so, but you also can implement the tactics to actually do it, right? Because a lot of you go to conferences and go to events that I really think are useless because you go there and you get, like, a motivation boner and then you don't have anything to do. It's serious. Like, you don't need to go to Tony Robbins, guys. Like, just be here. We have stuff specifically for you. So, where are you right now? Most of you are, this is so small, you can't see it, but on the Jim billionaire code, most of you come in or right now are selling owner or manager slash owner, okay? So, you come into Jim launch, Jim launch, and you're like, oh my god, this is so much crap, and you go through these 16 weeks and it completely changes your business and you as a person and challenges you more than anything that you thought was possible and you pay us more than anything you ever thought you would pay anybody, right? And so, at that point, typically you become a selling owner. This is typically, so I'd say 70% of you. So, you end up doing the sales because you don't have that ability to outsource it yet and you don't have yet the skill to translate your skills. And then those of you who do adopt that are managing owners. So, you're typically still in the location, you're overseeing people, you're the point of contact, but you're not, like, completely out of the business. I mean, jeez, I'm not even out of the business completely, you know, so I don't think anybody here would say they are. And what do you need at those points and what are you interested in and what am I talking about today is transformational leadership, culture, recruiting, and team development, right? So, like I just said, that's why you guys are so interested in this stuff is because Alex gives you all the stuff to solve sales and your front end issues and you have all of these options available there, right? So, you're not in a scarcity mindset when it comes to how you're going to feed yourself. But then you're like, holy crap, have all this inflow, how do I build an infrastructure to support it? And then when you're doing that, you're second guessing yourself a lot of the time because you're not sure what it's supposed to look like. So, the question really is what must you believe in in order to succeed now? Because what's so interesting is that the person I was when we built Gym Launch is not the person I have to be today to continue to run Gym Launch. Like, you have to learn to be adaptable as a person. Like, I would say even my personality, like, you guys love the disc chest, right? Who here loves the disc? You guys geek out over it. I've seen it on the Facebook group. I used to be like a super high DNI. And then ever since we started the business and then we started hiring people, since then, I went from having like a 10S and 10C to like a 55 on each. And then my DNI went down. And that's because I had to learn how to adapt those skills and become that person because what got me to where I was was not going to get me to the next level. And who you are that started the business, that like entrepreneur that's like crazy, falling and worked so hard and worked until you freaking puke, like, that's not going to get you to the next level of business. So, what you need to believe now, this was hard for me to come up with because I was like, what are like three beliefs that you have to believe until like, to make sure that you can do this. But I really think that these are the three things that I try to do. And I hope that you can at least take these away. And I want to instill these beliefs in you. So, one is you can't lead what you don't live. Two is that people trump strategy. And three is the best way to lose control is to try and control everything. So, I kind of want to explain what each one briefly. So, you cannot lead what you do not live. Why is this so important? Because the culture and the current state of your business is absolutely a reflection of you. Because what is at the top of the business is simply amplified as you grow. And so, I get to see this, I have the privilege of watching this as we grow because I see it as I develop as a leader, this drips down into the entire rest of the business. I see how it affects everybody, how I discipline the small group of people around me reflects into how everybody else does. Okay? So, what you are doing today is why your business is the way it is. So, you cannot complain and you cannot feel bad for yourself if you don't like your business or if you feel like it's not going to good state because you have complete control over it. So, it's interesting because it's really not a coincidence that those who are usually struggling in business have very messy emotional lives. And I can say this coming from somebody who was like that. Like, when we first started the business, we had lots of other things in our lives that we had to take care of in order to grow in business. So, it's also not a coincidence that so many of the people that are succeeding have very stable lives outside of their businesses, right? I'm sure this seems really obvious to most of you, but I kind of want to explain it in a different manner. Because what you do outside of your business is actually going to affect your business more than what you do inside of your business, right? Because what it's doing is it's stealing your attention. So, if you look at this, this was one of our mentors taught us this concept. He did it with this cool marble thing, but I can't do that anyways. So, either way, so say you have this much human attention, right? Like that big circle right there. That's all the human attention you have available. Imagine if you had that to put towards your business, right? But instead, where a lot of people end up is what limited attention looks like. And that's, you have attention on a failing marriage. You have attention on the fact that you're drinking too much. You have attention on the fact that you had a crappy childhood and you still feel bad for yourself. You have attention on the fact that you have 50K in debt. You have attention on the fact that you have this business. That's just one of the many things, okay? So, imagine like the strategic advantage you have if you actually have more attention. And so, that is why you, as the leader of your business, will always be the bottleneck. And so, the question is, you know, how do you stop being the bottleneck? And I want to preface this with whether you think drastically you are or not right now, you are always the bottleneck. I'm the bottleneck right now. Alex is the bottleneck right now. Because if we don't continue to grow and understand that we are always being the bottleneck, then the business will never grow. Because if we were, if we embodied what a billionaire was, we would be billionaires right now. It would be a billion-dollar company. But we are not there yet. So, we have to keep growing. How do you do this? I would say the main ways are you stop avoiding, dismissing, and excusing. So, you have to take a hard look in the mirror. It's not something easy to teach when it comes to self-awareness and taking a hard look in the mirror and saying, what do I suck at? Because you have to look at every situation, handle it, even though it's incredibly uncomfortable much of the time, but you have to do it in sequence and handle them. Alex and I had a lot of things that we had to rip open, they sucked. We had to point them out to each other and be like, this, you need to take care of this. Both of us had to do it to each other. And it was really hard. And we did this in a very short amount of time. But I can't tell you how those things that were outside of our business, handling those emotional distresses or whatever they may be, changed our business completely. So, that is what the ideal outcome is and that is what we are lucky enough to have right now, where it's ideal. Your focus is like your business and your family. And that's it. And imagine if you don't have that right now, how awesome that would be to have. So, second concept is people trump strategy. So, if you think about it like this, having all the strategy in the world for your business doesn't really matter if you don't have customers, right? We all know those people out there who are like, I'm an entrepreneur. And they're like, what do you do? And they're like, what have this idea for this thing? And you're like, dude, it's not freaking entrepreneur. Okay? Like, not true. It's the same if you don't have a team. So, if you're just that person going out there and you don't have anyone following you, like, it doesn't matter, right? But if you don't learn to translate your skills to others and grow them, your infrastructure is going to remain incredibly fragile because it's 100% dependent upon you, right? And the cool thing, if you guys don't have it, I saw that I had the hint part, but like, people will build the strategy for you, right? And so that's why it's so important to get those people into your business. Because otherwise, it looks like this, which is what a lot of you come into gym launch or gym legacy having, which is like, you feel like you're doing everything in the business. How many of you feel like that right now? Yeah. Don't do that. And a lot of you have said to me, you're like, Leila, but I just can't trust people. And we're going to talk about that because that's something in here that you need to work on, right? What it should look like is something to this effect, okay? Dave Regula showed me his org chart and he was like, he asked for some question. I think Alex messaged him one night and was like, I want your opinion on something because Dave's been with us for a while. And his org chart was similar to this, but they had a head coach. So it could be similar to this, but the idea is that you as the owner have somebody who's your right hand and that's protecting you. And so what you think of when you have an org chart is that each level protects the level above of it. Okay? So those people who can't just be doing their job, they should be thinking, how do I not let this get up there? How do I handle it well enough so that it doesn't leak up to the top? And like I said, the goal is to build people who build strategy and that becomes the highest use of your time. Once you have more than two people on your team, I would see when you have two people on your team. You might still be doing something, but you need to be prioritizing, pouring into them because ideally you're not going to be doing anything and instead you're just going to be helping others grow and learn. And how do you do this? You do this by delegating authority over tasks. This is a concept, I can't remember who it was, it was on a podcast. And I was like, oh, that's so good, authority over tasks. And this is how you would expand your org chart without pressure because what a lot of people do is they say, oh, you're in charge of sales. But then when it comes down to an actual decision about the sales department, they're not letting them make it. And so it's kind of like, okay, what do you have to do? You have to make sure that you're giving them decision-making power. If you give them tasks, you will create followers. So you'll create people who can go do things and they can go operate parts of the business. But if you give a thord, you're going to create leaders, which is the table we have of everyone in the back-and-gen launch. Those are people that we would consider on the strategic team that are important because they have decision-making power. And that's what you want to create. Because then that will, what that means is that when a problem arises in sales, you don't have to solve it. It's somebody else's responsibility. I can truly say, I don't think like Lily, like customer service, I don't think about customer service because every time I've ever brought anything to her eyes, she's like already handled it three days ago and I feel stupid. That's true. And that brings me to the third point, which is the best way to lose control is to try and control everything because it would literally break you as a human and as an entrepreneur. Because basically what you're doing is you're hiring people to just do things, but you're taking all the mental energy of making all the decisions and instead, like you're not saving your decision-making power and energy for the most important decisions about the business, which are strategic rather than task-oriented. So instead, this is Craig Gruchel that said this about the guarding your values part and then I twisted it with another part I heard and I was like, this is good. You need to guard your values so the values you uphold your business to, but you need to surrender your control. So what does that mean? You have to do it by repeating two words. You decide. So what I try very hard to practice is that every time someone emails me with a question or they ask me what we should do in a situation, regardless of if I think that they've thought it out completely themselves or not, or if they are like truly out of options and just need to ask me, I'm going to say something, well, I don't know, what do you think? Even if I think I have a great answer, there's been times when I do blurt it out and I'm like, crap, I should have said you need to pick. It's up to you. It's your decision, because that's how you delegate everything. You have to let other people decide. And so that's like pushing down decision-making and continually doing that. That is how you will outsource things. And you only make the decisions that only you can make. So this point is very true and I think it is why a lot of people have trouble delegating is because they're like, what if they mess up? They're going to freaking mess up. I have seen it. I have done it and we have it happen. People will royally mess up at times and they're going to do things that are like, you're like, I can't even believe that happened. How could they forget to do that? You feel like baffled by it because you're from your perspective. But you have to just let it happen because people do not learn if they are not given the ability to fail. And that is what the growing pains are. So if you want to grow, you're going to experience the pains of it. There are the mile markers basically telling you that you're on your way there. And if you're not experiencing the discomfort and pain, then you're not getting closer to your goal and there's probably something that you're not doing that you know you should. So part two, I want to go over what we call now like the five core functions internally of the business. I think this will, because Alex talks about it so much when it comes to externally, I like to flip it and look at it internally. And what I want you all to understand is that what you do when it comes to the marketing and the sales of building the business itself essentially is the same thing that you're going to do internally to build your team. So you have the five external core functions, which is lead gen, lead nurture, sales fulfillment, resell ascension. So what does that look like for you guys? A lot of the times it's lead gen is challengers or members, lead nurture, ALN scheduling, calling, sales, get the money, fulfillment, deliver amazing service, and then resell ascension, HDP membership, semi. Five internal, lead gen is applicants, lead nurture is calling, scheduling, checking their social media, sales is selling them on the company vision, fulfillment is treating them amazingly well, and then resell ascension is management and coaching. So it's essentially if your mind works like Alex's, he likes to see things in the same way externally, internally, and it really helps. So there is lead gen and there are lead nurture, which I made the decision not to talk about today because they are more tactical and we're providing that in what we're releasing in two weeks anyways, and it would just be me telling you to click a bunch of buttons and put up this ad of this and that, and I don't want to do that. So I'm going to spend my time where people actually need the help, which is starting at number three, which is sales. So sales is going to be from the interview to the post initial onboarding. So just like you guys sell people on the vision of weight loss, you need to sell people on what life after they take this job would look like. And I said this at the last event, that line, and then what I'm about to tell you, but I want you all to hear it because some of you weren't there, it's worth seeing twice, and that is probably what I would quote my smartest thing I've ever came up with on my own, is that you have two types of customers. One is the one that you pay, and then number two are the ones that you pay. I just said the same thing, didn't I? Whatever. You guys get what I'm saying. And the thing is that a lot of people come back at me and they're like, that's so employee focused, and I hear it a lot, especially from people who have been, they have the disadvantage that they have been in the market for a very long time, and they have had jobs for years and years and years, and so they're used to the old way it worked to manage people, right? What's cool for me, because I've never done it before, so I see what I think works with the generations that are looking for jobs now, and that's what I do. And so the question is, if you're thinking that's too focused on my team, like I don't want to baby them, they're people that I pay, who here has had a client who you sold a challenge to, and they remain skeptical the entire time. So imagine what happens when you hire somebody, but you didn't sell them on the vision. You literally get that as an employee. So why would you not sell them on the vision? Do you want that the rest of the time? I mean, like, I've literally seen it. It's terrible. You have that person that goes in the back of the office, they start talking crap about you, they talk to the clients about you, all that. That means they're not sold on the vision. So let's talk about what selling them on the vision looks like. So I would call this an ideal employee sales cycle. So this is from hiring to onboarding, and it's small slides, so if you guys want to screenshot them, you can, but this will be provided in what we're releasing on the 15th of June. One, they go to apply and see a video of you pitching the position and read the description. They feel called by it like this is the position for me. Two, they see testimonials from your business and social proof, voicing how much you care and realize you're a genuine person who has a client's best interest in mind. Three, they google you and on Facebook see oodles of testimonials and five stars of reviews, more social proof. Four, they get a call from you that does not sound fake and phony, reminding them that they have an interview. They arrive at the interview to someone who's well dressed and clean and not answering their phone, and they're very friendly, they're not exhausted or distracted. They hear your back story for why you started the gym, got into the industry, and they relate to you and your purpose. They read your client-centric mission and core values and just know this job is for them and they give you examples of why. They get a call from a very excited owner offering them the job, followed by an email that day with all the official letters and paperwork. The next day after signing the paperwork, they get a super friendly welcome email explaining them what to expect from the onboarding, as well as some get to know you packets so that the team can get to know them. Two days later, they receive a card and a welcome swag bag with team gear, welcoming them aboard. A week before starting, they get another email with their week one and day one agendas as well as their point of contact, credentials, directory, org chart, communication cycle. They show up on their first day to a big greeting on your daily huddle from the whole team followed by a touch base with the supervisor to go over their day one. And then their first week is spent getting to know the team, chatting in classes, meeting members, and learning more of the core material to get them prepared to be a part of the team. So if you execute all those steps and you have done everything right on your end to have a person who is the right fit, 100% bought in on your mission, and I know what you're thinking, you're like holy crap, that's a lot of stuff and that's a lot of information, right? And even if I do everything right, how do I even know that they're a good fit for the job? Like how do I know? Because there's two sides to the interviewing, right? Number one rule about hiring is that you need to be logical, okay? So hiring is logical, emotions are complicated, be logical, okay? And so that's, I would say in business in general, that's something you need to focus on, but especially when it comes to hiring. And so how do you know that you have the right answers to make this right decision? It's going to take practice for one, because I know some of you are like how do you guys do it? I'm like dude, I've had a couple crazy people, like we've been taken to an unemployment core or whatever you want to call it, like you're going to mess up, like it's okay, like it happens, but what you need to understand is that you can't let that, like if you tell me you're scarred from something like that, like dude, get out of business then because that's what's going to happen. People are going to scar you over and over again, you're having people that you're like, they were so normal and you trusted them and they're going to stab you in the back, it's going to happen all the time. But if you can sum up in one word, what an awesome interview would look like so that you can avoid this and not have those people, I would say it would be authentic. And so I know that's easier said than done, how do you get authentic in an interview, because that's like a word right now, like be authentic, but you exemplify it, right? So my team like jokes about this, being one of my best abilities, so I feel really confident talking about it, well here it is, it's like the simplest thing in the world, okay? All I do is I talk to the people, like I would just get to know anyone else on the phone and I share stuff with them about my personal self and like my personal life and like my background and things like that, right? Then I start to ask them questions, like I have an interview sheet of like 30 questions, I'll just pick one that seems right, and then I'm going to stop and I'm going to shut up and I'm going to ask more questions and I'm going to dig into what they're saying, I'm going to say what else, can you tell me more, what else, can you tell me more, I'm just going to see what comes out, right? And then I'm going to ask more follow-up questions and then I'm going to shut up and listen, because a couple of you have talked about the interviews that you've conducted and it sounds like you're doing more of the talking than the listening, well you should be getting to know the person, if you do all the talking then you don't know anything about them when you leave, they just know that you talk a lot. So most importantly, this is contrary to a lot of what people preach, but like do not rehearse your interviews, I think I followed some of this when we first started of like answering, asking the same questions over and over and doing this, but like people are different, people are different for the positions, people are different for the roles, etc, etc, they're generally all different, so I don't rehearse interviews, because if you rehearse and practice and plan your interviews, you know what you're going to get as answers from those people on your interviews, you're going to get rehearsed, practice, and planned answers, because they have a canned answer for everything, I get on the phone with people and one of the last girls I interviewed she was like, man, she was like, I practiced, practiced, like don't you want to know that we're like compatible, but people are so concerned with getting the job that they practice what they're going to tell you, so it's like their best canned answer, like if you don't want that, then you have to be relaxed with them, you have to let them understand that you're like a human, you're a person, and then you're going to get that back from them, and once you dig in and you say like, what else tell me more, and you're going to get past those canned answers if you ask enough times, and you're going to get the real answer, okay, there's been multiple times where someone gets to my level of interviewing, and I keep doing that, and I get them to tell me that they were like in jail, or like they hated their last 10 employers, and they literally go through one, it was actually seven, seven employers, and she told me why each one of them sucked, right, and like our team is smart, like they do that stuff, but like you really have to dig in sometimes to get to know someone, so the second way of knowing is your post interview reflection, and this is just literally being self-aware, and a lot of people don't do this, because what it is is it's hard, and a lot of people want to go fast, and they want to hire fast, so they don't stop to ask themselves these questions, okay, but logic is going to ask what's required of the role, what are the strengths of the applicant, is this role specifically the best for this person's strengths, and does this person fit the culture, so why I like that is because what's required of the role, is it sales, okay, sales, what are the strengths of the applicant, are they good at selling, cool, they're good at selling, is the strength, is this role specifically the best fit, well nobody else sells, so yes, that's the best fit, right, and then does this person fit the culture, like do you genuinely like being around them, or do you like, you're like oh they could totally do the job, but you're like would you hang out with them, no, then no, and I just wanted to put this as like a warning, because that seems like so generalized, and like okay, self-awareness questions, but like seriously if you're sitting here and you're thinking to yourself, oh that doesn't make a difference, it's like four questions, I don't need to ask myself that, like the blah blah, that is the difference between what makes someone who hires a great team versus an okay team, and so if you're thinking that you need to do this more than anyone, don't be lazy, just ask yourselves the questions after you do the interviews and reflect upon it, and then based on these questions, you should have an idea of whether you should or should not hire someone, I wanted to give some more specific examples since people love examples of like what the scenarios might look like, right, so reasons to hire someone would be their strengths match the requirements of the role, the rest of your team gets along with them or genuinely feels like they would be a good addition, so like a lot of the time someone gets past it to me, and I know not by what my team says, but how they act, like their demeanor when they talk about the person, if they're good fit, because it'll be like oh yeah hey Leila, like you have uh you have Shirley on Thursday for an interview, and I'm like how was she, and they're like oh she was good, I'm like okay, and then I'm like hey Leila you have that you have Shirley on Thursday for an interview, okay, I'm like was she good, oh yeah she was good, like that's the difference right, so I know by how they're talking to me, they ask a lot of questions about the company, the future, the morale, the biggest one for me that's interesting is when someone doesn't ask about that, I'm like dude if you're about to like sign essentially the biggest relationship of your life to the company, why would you not ask like where's the company going, don't you want like stability and like things like that, and you want someone who's long-term minded in that aspect, they're highly referred from your clients or staff, so anyone that is recommended by you guys, like we will always consider, same with internally from our staff, we will always consider, because that typically means that they have similar values to someone who's already on the team, right, someone who explains why they want this job particularly over all the rest in the running, I've gotten that from a couple of like the best people that we've brought on, they have told me why they have offers from other places, or they almost started with other places, but then they felt called when they saw our ad, and then they were like I have to give that a chance, right, and those are some of the best people because they were that attracted to our ad, and then they share the same values with you and the rest of the team, so when you go over like what you value in your team, they jive with it, they're like dude that's totally like on my level, and like you don't feel any friction there, reason not to hire somebody, if you're desperate and just need someone to start like yesterday, which I know a lot of people feel like they're in that position, they're like dude I'm working like freaking 16 hours a day or whatever, and I'm selling, and I'm fulfilling the classes, and I'm trying to do the billing and oversee this and that, and I just hired two new trainers, and so you're going to try and go through it a lot faster, and the funny thing is a lot of times you actually sell yourself on the fact that you're not going that fast, and this person is a good fit, when in reality you're just selling yourself on that, okay, so you have to really take time to sit and think about it, if they're over qualified for the position, this is something that I've had a lot of people ask me, that well I want them to quit their job, where they make you know 100 plus 100 plus grand a year, I'm like what are you ever gonna have that's gonna pay that much to someone, do you get paid that much, so you need to confirm that you have a position for someone today, and if they're looking for something long-term in the future, right, and so if they're over qualified, that could almost be like an underutilization of an eagle is almost worse than hiring a bad person, because that person is going to be bored, they ask zero questions, it's obviously weird, they did no research on your facility, I had somebody who didn't even remember the company name on an interview, I was like you're joking, and so I was like I don't have time for this bye, your co-workers don't like them or don't think they're a good fit, so a lot of the times if somebody tells me they don't be like I don't know, I just got a funny feeling when I talked to them, then that's probably not a good fit, they're qualified, and on paper, and when you talk to them you're like I think one of our teammates actually said this to me, she goes yes you can do the job, and I was like that does not sound exciting, I don't want to hang out with some person that can just do the job, I want someone who like the whole team gets along with and they add value to the culture of the company, and lastly someone who badmouths all their prior places of work, which the interesting part is that they won't, you have to look for the difference, there's a difference between badmouthing and then saying that because they became self-aware of what they're good versus not good at, like oh I realized after I joined like it wasn't a good fit because I actually like doing X more, that's fine, that's great, that tells you the stuff of where, but if it's someone who just badmouths that place, you do not want that, so once you've made that decision, I went over this a lot more on the last, at the last event, but for your onboarding templates and sequences, this has been something I've been working on that you guys will have on 6.15 when we release the first part of the portal, but the main thing that you have to understand is that once you've made that decision to hire these people, the mistake that a lot of people make is they don't put in all the effort in the beginning, right, and I remember talking to Alex Charfin about this when we were first hiring people and I like really took it overboard, I think it was Yasmin, my executive assistant, I talked to her twice a day for like 45 minutes each, but she's like the best hire ever because I invested a ton of time into her just getting to know her as a person, letting her get to know me, and then slowly offloading things, so in the short term, it should take more of your time, but in the long term, it will reap its benefits. Number four is fulfillment, so switching to post hiring and onboarding, so looking at this from an external perspective, customer fulfillment, which you guys all talk about and are really used to, is providing great services that can satisfy your customer's needs, right? Employee fulfillment is the same thing, especially like guys, I can't stress this enough, like if you learn from your parents, like how to fulfill in the workplace or you read these like old, old-ass books, like that's not what works nowadays because people are looking for a different kind of work culture, they're not looking for just like clock in, clock out, like people are looking for purpose and work, and so I want to break it down to what I see it as, and it's employee needs are just human needs, and it's just providing it in one place, just like you guys trying to do within your gym and make an ecosystem where people want to be, it's the same with your team, so physiological needs would translate to steady pay and work-life boundaries, right? Safety needs, security and freedom from fear, belonging needs, belonging part of a group, relationships, esteem needs, mastery and status, and then self-actualization, which is personal growth and challenges. So how do you fulfill on these? First, we'll talk about physiological, which is pay and boundaries, okay? This is the simplest of all. One, pay what you promised and pay on time, and then two, oh man, it resized. I'm sure you guys have seen the book by Simon Sinek, Leaders Eat Last, so I do agree with like if it comes down to it, your team will eat before you, I mean when we first started our business, like a bunch of crap went wrong, we had to pay out people like a lot of money that we didn't have, and we had to take it from, it was just not fun. We had no money, but we were paying it out of our own pockets from our personal funds to make sure that our team could at least eat, and they felt okay, and that's your job as the owner. I've seen people have, it's happened twice now in the group where I've just seen it where they're like, do I just not pay them? Do I wait? I'm like, oh my god, your team's gonna never trust you ever again, do not do that, like find a way, you can find a way. Okay, four boundaries. One, on your days off and on their days off, just don't contact them. So this is something that especially like when we started growing the team became like, because in the beginning, you're small, it's just like you and one other person, you guys talk kind of all the time, but as you get bigger, you really need to respect these boundaries, otherwise people don't feel like they can actually ever leave if they do need, because even us as owners, like you need to date off. Okay, so on their days off, do not contact them, otherwise don't expect them not to contact you on your days off. Reach out to them as little as possible between your scheduled communication. So something that I have worked on a lot in the last couple months is that I make sure that anything that is not urgent can wait until I have a weekly touch base with somebody, or it can wait until the daily huddle, because I don't need to voxer them about something that is not immediate. And then set communication expectations. Okay, so communication expectations how are you going to communicate, when are you going to communicate, what media, and that's something we built a template for, because that's really important to know, like how can you use Facebook, how can you use texting, like if you guys are like, my trainer texted me at eight o'clock at night, I'm like, did you ever tell them not to text you at eight o'clock at night? Like, unless you've set it and it's written down somewhere, don't expect them to know. Safety, which is freedom from fear. Why should you care about this? Because if you don't, your business will not grow and you will continue to be the bottleneck for it. Because it's very interesting, the quickest way to create a bad employee is to be the scary boss, because what fear does is it immobilizes employees, it stops people from being able to move, whether they know what to do or not, they don't want to, because they don't want to poke the bear. So unless you want to spend your days doing this, which is assigning tasks, checking on tasks, and fixing tasks, which I bet a lot of you do right now, you need to learn to create safety and predictability. So what does that mean? That means if you want safety and predictability from your team, you've got to give it to them. And that is the exact opposite of what a lot of you have had to do to start a business. You start a business being unpredictable, taking a huge risk that's not safe, and now you have this team who requires you to then go back and become safe and predictable again. So it's almost like why you got out of a job, because you hated the predictability and the monotonous day in, day out, or whatever you were doing before, is what you need to actually learn to have more of when you build a team. How do you implement this? This is stuff that we've put in the portal that we're going to be releasing, but these are the fundamentals. It's just easy stuff. A daily huddle, a weekly meeting, weekly or bi-weekly, one-on-ones with somebody. Clear expectations and visions. So what does that mean? That means they have job expectations like what are you expected to do, what are your responsibilities, what's your communication cycle, and they have monthly meetings, because monthly meetings are more about like, where are we taking it this month to go towards our long-term vision? And those are honestly the most basic things that will yield that ROI. But what you need to remember is that number one is that you as a person are consistent and predictable, because like we talked about in the beginning, right, it's like everything from the top is amplified and it drips down. So what does that mean? That means be on time. Don't be emotional and do what you say you will do. So just like you should expect people to give you commitments and then follow through and have them done next week or whatever it may be, you need to do the same for your team. So when you say you're going to do something, you need to do it. Don't be emotional. I think that is probably one of the hardest things for people who are entrepreneurs, but you have to remember for your team, you need to be the neutral ground. And so it's a practice. I practice, I have a little note that says be neutral. So that when I'm on a meeting and someone tells me something that pisses me the f off, or they say something, I'm like, I'm just like... And I've really worked on it. Like they could probably say like it was not like that in the beginning. I'd be like, what the fuck? But it takes practice. Like it doesn't mean just because you're this way in the beginning that you have to stay that way, like we're people we can change. And then being on time. A lot of people talk about like trainers late, trainers this, trainers that, that's just drips down from the top. I'm sure there was a time whether it be a month ago or 11 months ago that you were late. And so that became acceptable. And so what it really comes down to is that this is doing yourself a favor because nobody worth hiring is going to work for someone that they don't admire in one way, shape, or form. So AKA live what you lead, be a person worthy of having followers, right? What would a person insanely worthy of having like a magnificent team? What would they do? What would they look like? What would they embody? Belonging needs, relationship and group. So one is the relationship with the company. And then two is their relationship with you. Now you strengthen both of these by showing appreciation and giving honest feedback. So there's two parts to it. And as a business owner, I really do believe that the most valuable way you can show appreciation is by giving your time to the team. I have never gotten a better thank you from someone or better felt more appreciated than when I've given them time to talk to them. Like we give prizes, gift cards, whatever, stuff like that. I've never gotten as much appreciation as I do when I give someone my time. And so what does that look like? A big part is weekly one-on-ones, which I know a lot of you are like, I do a one-on-one or whatever. There's a difference between it being person focused and task focused. So I know that one of my one-on-ones every week is always going to be task focused because we're going over projects and this and that and it's like turn and burn, let's go. But the rest of them I know are always person focused, which is really just like you giving a shit about the person. And that's why you have to like your team because you can't give a shit about people you don't like. So you need to do this before all else. And I think this is, a lot of people see, like Alex talked about, we do gifts and we have a holiday calendar of what we give gifts for and what's the budget and all those things. But none of that matters, you don't actually care about them because if you don't care about your team, then when they get that stuff they're going to be like, bullshit, like stupid. That's when you start to look like politics and corporate. So the icing on top once you actually care and you have a team that you want to lead is doing all that stuff. You celebrate birthdays, you celebrate when they get a new house, when they get married, when they have a baby, you send them cards when someone dies or a pet dies, like sending them company swag, sending them personal handwritten cards, announcing wins to the whole team, sending them books or buying them courses to enhance their skill set. Those are things that you do for people you care about, right? So do them for your team. And I'm breaking it down like this into such a like, like that. But innately, I want to do those for our team because I genuinely like everyone on the team. So I sometimes have to hold myself back because I'm like, oh, I want to get them more. I see something online, oh, she would love that. And I'm like, that's a little weird. You don't need to do that because I really like our team and I think they're awesome. So that's, I had this slide on the last one, but like little things you can do, like get someone a new pair of headphones, send them something for a holiday, get them a book that will help them in their skill set, send them a card. We have a welcome to the team, you know, like the card from, there's an app for that. I'll remember. But like I said, do not think that this stuff and the gifts are like how you build a good culture within it. This is just, it's the icing on top of it because the real way to make someone feel like they belong is to give them a job where they can see and feel their contribution to the company. So they see the long-term vision and they feel appreciated in what they contribute on a daily basis. And that's what top performers look for anyways. So people who are poor performers are going to look for all the like perks and things like that, like, oh, what are the paid holidays and benefits and you guys do like, you know, Friday socials or anything like that's bullshit. Like people who are top performers, they don't care about any of that. They care about like, am I going to get stretched? Am I going to fulfill these needs? Like, am I going to work hard? Am I going to feel like I'm contributing? Which brings you kind of to mastery and status. So status, the same way that you give status to your clients is really how you should be giving it to your team. You guys do it with your clients. I see it all the time in the group. So like, why not do it with your team? Example, like prizes for getting X amount of referrals, awards for teaching X number of sessions, public recognition for going above and beyond with the client and someone like saves the day, you want to make sure they get recognition for it publicly. Mastery. So the same way that your clients want to feel competent yet challenged, you have the same dance you have to do with your team, right? So examples. Achievement would be like completing six months of programming for the team, right? That's something that like a lot of you are like, man, who does the program? And there's that like, give that to someone and see if they can do it. And if it's awesome, like you don't need to like say it's permanent, but like give people the opportunity to grow stretch goals, say like, dude, like, I'm going to challenge you this month to sign up 30 members of EFT. Alex did that with our sales team. He's like, sign up 80 legacy in a month. And we're all like, what? You know, and like, they hit it. Like it's insane what people will hit if you give them a goal, but they need someone to set those paths for them. More responsibility. These are two of my favorite of more responsibility and more autonomy, which would be more responsibility when someone's in a position they're like, why want more responsibility? I'm like, well, that's pretty much a position, but the next part after you've learned and you've mastered something is teaching other people to do it, right? So then I'm like, okay, cool. I would love if you can help with the hiring and training process, right? And like, that's going to take something off your plate. It's something that they can help you with. And it's going to help them grow in their role and as a person. And then more autonomy. So something like giving them first round of interviews to do. And then even we've gotten to the point now where it's like, okay, you guys, let me know if you think we should hire that person. You know? That's what we've recently gotten to. So you're giving them more authority by relinquishing control over the lower level decisions so that then you can soon move up to the higher level decisions and soon you are only making strategic decisions. And so what I want you to notice is that what this means is that you have to extend trust to the teammate if you're going to let them take on more. And so a lot of people ask, how do I know if I can trust someone? You freaking trust them. Like, guys, I am, that is one of the things that Alex taught me so well. My husband is like, he just trusted me. And I was like, he has no reason to trust me. He like gave me a bank account information the first week we met to like help him process stuff. And I was like, fuck. Right? I'm like, that's a little far. But either way, you know, how do you know you can trust someone? You give them trust and then you see what happens. Like, that's all. Like, you can't be scarred by people or employees or anything that have done you wrong because it's going to happen over and over again. But the only way you find out is you get better at the process and then you keep trying. And it is inevitable that somebody is going to break it. And like it makes you, you're not special, you're not the special snowflake that they were talking about. I think it was that yesterday. Yes. It doesn't make you a special snowflake. Like, you do not have trust issues because someone quit or like did something wrong. Like, that happens to all of us. Self-actualization. So growth and full potential, which kind of ties in with the mastery and status. But I want you to think about it like this. The job of a mentor is to be so confident of the student that the student uses that borrowed confidence to reach their full potential. So what the best mentors do is they challenge those around them to act even when they are afraid that they will fail. Relevant example. Alex. How many times has he persuaded you guys to do something that you probably didn't think possible, didn't know existed or were nervous or scared to do? That's what a mentor does. That's what a leader does. And honestly, he doesn't so our entire team. He sets standards higher than anyone would ever do. Even for me. Like, it takes me, it's usually like, Alex has this crazy standard he wants to set. Layla is like, oh my God. And then I take like a day to like absorb it. And then I'm like, we can do this. We can do this. And then I'm like, all right, Mimi. And that's like the next barrier. I'm like, Mimi, okay, this is this thing. And she's like, I don't know. And then it like takes her a couple of days. And then she's like, okay. And then she tells the team and the team's like, all right, well, if Layla and Mimi are on board, like, we're good, right? But the whole point is that he sets those standards and then challenges us to become the people that we need to be to fulfill and to meet those standards. Right. And that's what leading is. Oh, rule number one. I don't know if I skipped or something. Ah, yes. Okay. Number five. Reselling ascension. So this is management and coaching. And rule number one is you cannot lead what you do not live. You guys see the theme here, right? That's like the number one rule. That's the number one belief. And so I wanted to highlight this because these are some things I see and I want you to understand. You cannot expect your team to be on time if you're not. You cannot expect your team to dress well if you do not. In fact, interesting one here. I used to dress like crap and I didn't do my hair and makeup. And as I've started to every day in the last couple of months, all of a sudden I've noticed four to five members of the team who do the same just by me doing it. Do not expect your team to give a shit about your business if you do not. So if you act like you don't care, even if you do, if you act like you don't, they don't care. Do not expect them to be organized if you're not. If you're not organized and you're like, my team's so disorganized, like, what? Do not expect them to be emotionally stable if you are not. If you're up and down all the time, your team's going to be up and down. Do not expect them to be positive if you are not. Do not expect them to appreciate you if you don't appreciate them. Do not expect them to train amazing sessions if you don't train amazing sessions. Like, you don't have excuses as the owner. You might be like, I'm more tired. I do more. It does not matter. You have, by choosing to own this business and open this gym, you have chosen to have higher standards and be held to a higher level than anybody else on the team and you have to be the first one to hold yourself to that. And then do not expect your team to honor your clients if you do not honor your clients. So if you're talking about customer service and how they're acting on the back end, you really need to take a look in the mirror and say, what am I not doing customer service wise? And we all lie to ourselves, guys. Like, I do it too. I have to dig deep and really try to get people, like, I love when someone gives me negative feedback in the company, like something critical, like, oh, well, this sucks. And I'm like, oh, my God, thank you. Because a lot of people, one, we have blind spots that we are, even as self-aware as we grow, you're going to have blind spots and people can help you get there faster by telling you, right? Like, it's help. But your actions are really what set the standard for your entire team. And in order to manage your team, you must first successfully manage yourself. So some of you have read Managing One's Self by Peter Drucker. I read it like four or five years ago. I was like, this is so good. It's so simple, but it's so true. Because management is really not about telling someone to do something, watching them while they do it, and correcting them on what they did. Management is about giving them a clear outcome and extending trust to your team on how to get there. So you want to give clarity and extend trust. So those are the two currencies of empowerment. So this is a universal example that I got from Craig Groucho when he was talking about this, because he preaches. But Jesus. So we all pretty much know the story of the Bible, whether religious or not. So what did he do? He identified 12 men with little experience. He invested his life in them. He empowered them and believed in them. He spent three years with them. And then he told them to go change the world, go and make disciples of all nations. Right? So what did he do? He communicated with clarity. What? Go and make disciples. Where? All nations. When? Now and for the rest of life. But where's the how? How? You have to extend trust and let them decide how. And that's the hardest thing for anyone to do, because most owners make up excuses in their head as to why they specifically cannot manage this way. Their team specifically, they can't run this way. Whatever it may be. Right? And the fact is, it's actually just you being lazy, because it's easier to micromanage than it is to trust someone who can and may fail you. Or to make the decision that they shouldn't be on your team. And in order to really lead, you have to have both of these currencies. You can't have one or the other. Because clarity without trust produces fear and inaction. Because they're like, okay, I know where we're going, but I don't feel like this leader trusts me, so I don't even want to go that way. Because I feel like they're going to just criticize every step I take. Right? It causes them to freeze. And then trust without clarity produces work without direction. So what's that look like? It's like when you're like, why did they do that? They literally spent four hours doing what? Organizing what file? Like, what the heck are you doing? And you're thinking of all the stuff that you need to be doing to get towards the goal you have. But if they don't know what that goal is, then they don't know what to prioritize. But most people are smart enough to be able to reverse engineer if they do know what that goal is. And so what clarity will do is it will ensure that your team members' work is aligned with your goals and mission. Right? So if they know how to contribute to the big picture and how necessary their work is, they'll typically do the right things in the right order. And then trust is the necessary net that results in the risk taking. Right? It's like, because you believe in me, I can try. I have the freedom to create, innovate, try again, and fail. Right? So the question that I'm sure a lot of you are asking in your head right now is like, what if I don't trust the people I work with? What if I have a team and I don't trust them? And you're telling me all this, but I just don't feel it. Then either you are the wrong leader or you have the wrong team. And either way, it's a problem that you have to solve. I like that one. Coaching. So talking about management and coaching, right? So coaching is actually mostly comprised of just giving helpful feedback. So without timely and honest feedback, your growth is always going to be limited. And this is where a lot of us struggle, myself included. Because no matter how self-aware you are, you have blind spots. Timely is going to be when, and then honest is how. So you want to think about it like this. If the first time that you give someone feedback is during a performance review, you are too late. I think the first time we ever did a performance review, I remember I was like, oh man, I have some things to say to her. And I was like, why did I do that? That was really too late, right? Because if you let it get that far, it's not timely and it's really not honest because you're not telling it in the appropriate time. So you need to normalize giving feedback and give it immediately. Now you're probably thinking, when do I not give feedback? Some parts where you don't give feedback is right before they have to perform. So like, for an example, somebody said something or right when they're in like a meeting presentation, at the last mastermind, one of our teammates said something to me that was not appropriate. And I was in front of actually a lot of you and I was like, Ash walked away. I had a meeting with her two weeks later and I was like, listen, this is why that wasn't okay, blah, blah, etc. Because I'm not going to do that in front of when we have all you here for the event. And then in public, you don't need to give them feedback when it has nothing to do with the rest of the team. Most people, I've done it. I think Mel, I did it to you once. And she was like, I do not respond well to that. And I was like, I am so sorry. You don't want to give it in front of the rest of the team if it has nothing to do with them. Now what about how? How do you give feedback? First off, you want to explain what's not happening. So a lot of times when I meet with somebody to have one on one and they're not expecting it or they don't really know what it's about necessarily, I'm like, hey, this probably freaked you out. Like, you're not getting fired or losing your job or anything. Like, you're okay. And then they're like, whew. And so what that does is it disarms your team member because you've eliminated their biggest fear, which is that you're going to cut them and they're going to lose their job. Right? So it brings their guard down. Now, how do you actually deliver the feedback you have to give them if you're like, man, Nancy literally sucks at sessions. Like, Nancy cannot deliver sessions. Nancy, every time she's in a session, she's her body language is like this. And she's chewing gum and she checks her phone all the time and you're like, how do I get this feedback? Well, you need to give them one to two areas of improvement. Don't tell her every single thing that she does wrong in the session plus the five things outside of that. Pick one to two things that will bring the most ROI to their performance and then tell them exactly what they can do to improve. So somebody who's really good at this on our team is Mimi, who's our COO, and she's very good at being like Layla because you also have to manage up. So she'll tell me if she has feedback for me exactly what it is, exactly what she thinks might work better. And it's a lot easier to digest that way because you're like, okay, that wasn't ideal, but this is. Right? Alex is the same thing. And in most cases, when you're delivering the feedback, it is good to explain to the person that though their intention was good, it did not have the impact that they were desiring. Right? So like there's multiple times. It's like rarely does someone actually want to make a negative impact on your business, especially if they're working for you. But intention doesn't always equal impact. So it's good to explain, especially when it's an awesome team member, be like, hey, like when you did this, like, I know that you were trying for this, but look what it did. Like you didn't want that. And so lastly, I actually think this one is the most important, is how do you speak to them about it? Right? One, it's where they can improve. It's not what they did wrong. Right? You don't bash them for doing something wrong. You talk about where they have room to improve. Okay? People take that much more positively. And then this is like the biggest one, and if I could just get you to take away anything from this when you're getting feedback, people will hear your words, but they feel your attitude. So it does not matter. Nobody gives a shit what you're saying if they feel like you're a dick. If you're talking to them and you can see that you're angry. Guys, like people can see when they're in front of you, we see it on Zoom. I can see their body language, how they get uncomfortable. I say something. If I say something that they weren't expecting me to like ask them that question, and they kind of like twitch a little, I'm like, oh, they're lying right now. Like, you know, like you're smart and emotionally aware. And so when you come in to give feedback to somebody, you cannot be in this A-hole state. Like, you have to be calm. You have to be neutral because they're not going to take your feedback unless you give it in a good manner, which is like not being mean to them. Like, be nice. You know? So that was a lot. And a lot of the leadership training and legacy will have more detailed explanations as we release it over the next couple months. The fundamentals and the foundational pieces are going to be released next Friday, I believe. And I want you guys to know the reason that I contributed to this is because I saw that as we were growing so quickly, what we needed was like specified training for our team on how to run Gym Watch. And as I was building it, I was like, dude, this is the same stuff for the gym. It just needs to be tweaked a little. And so I know I've reached out to a couple of you for help and like, okay, take this concept. Like, how are you using it right now? Like, what is your biggest struggle right here? Did I miss something here? But it was like, I basically have been building this internal leadership training for our team. And then I've been able to take it and flip it and use it so that you guys can have it as well. So it's been really fun to work on. But I want to recap a couple things that I want you guys to have as takeaways, which is, one, the new beliefs you should walk out here with, which is you cannot lead what you do not live. And yes, that means having more self-discipline and holding yourself to a higher standard. It's hard. Like, business is not easy. It is hard. Two is that people trump strategy, because your business will not move. You will not be able to reach, you know, whatever it may be as your goal to be one of the best gyms and legacy if you do not have the people to help build your business to get there. And three is that the best way to control, to lose control is to try and control everything. And then a couple of the things we covered. The five external functions are reflected internally. That hiring needs to be logical. Onboarding and hiring for your team is the sale. Your authenticity is going to trigger the authenticity of others, especially when it comes to interviewing. Employee fulfillment is the same as all the human needs, which is just meeting all six of those. And then to manage, you extend trust and give clarity. And lastly, is that coaching is a consistent cycle of feedback. So I think we have 30 minutes. That was perfect. We have 30 minutes for Q&A if we want to. So if you guys want to ask questions on what I just covered, then now would be the time. Hello. Oh, this is fun. So I often get feedback from my team. Then they tell me, Beth, you're too nice. You're too nice. You're too nice. Can you speak on that? I mean, like the last few slides, you know, like don't be an A-hole. Got it. But what if you're too nice? Like getting run over. So your team that tells you that, are they the ones taking the feedback from you? Or is it just other members of your team? No, other members of the team watching interactions between either me and clients or me and my other staff. I guess the question is, does it yield a result when you have those interactions or does it not? The interactions with... Yeah, so they say that you're being too nice, but like when you have that interaction with that person, do they change their behavior? How you want? Or is it consistently not working? More the latter. Consistently not working. Okay. Then are you telling them the truth? Like if someone's about to be fired, or if you think a client is not a good fit, are you telling them that kind of harsher truth? A lot of times I will allow behavior. I think a lot of times I'll allow behaviors too much. And so then at the end of it, it's like, okay, I finally need to cut this off. Yeah. No, I've made that mistake. It's really something that takes practice. And so you just, the best thing you can do is like right now, this is actually my main focus, like the book I'm reading is called Thanks for the Feedback, because I'm like, I think I need to be better at giving feedback and giving it more often and giving it this, right? So it's a huge focus of mine, because I thought I was good at it, but what I was good at to get to where we are now with 50 people is not going to get me higher, right? I would say the best thing that you can do is test how you talk to the people. And what you need to remember, like bottom line is that feedback is just something that you have to give someone to help them. Like don't look at it as good or bad. What would help them do better? It's going to be different for each person. How I give me, me feedback is not how I give Mel feedback or Alex feedback. They're all different people. And so some of the people that I talk to, I have to be kind of harsh because they don't understand, unless I am harsh. That's what they respond to and they know it too. But then some people have to be nice to because they're so hard on themselves already that like, what I say means nothing, because like in here, they're like, I suck. I'm a failure. Totally get that. Like, I think it was back, like back, I would say back in August, like I didn't feel like I had any like ability to even speak on this stuff to people, right? But then it really comes down to like you, when you, when they relinquish that trust to you, you have to then say, okay, this person's giving me that trust. So like, you should, you then have to kind of step into this new identity, which is not easy, but it's kind of like when people are losing weight, like they're, they're going from like, I'm a fat person to like, I'm, I'm a healthy bitch. You know what I mean? Like, you're stepping in this new role. And it's the same thing, like you go from like, I am so-and-so's helper to like, I am the freaking boss. Like, I'm a boss lady or like, whatever you have to say to yourself, you know what I mean? It's really the same concept. And so you just have to affirm to yourself over and over again, what that is that you want to become. I mean, I literally, I'm, I'm the person who does the self affirmations. Like, I'm, I'm gonna see you with Jim launch. Like, come on, Layla, like walking on my treadmill in the morning. I would be on the treadmill. I'm like, I'm gonna see you over a million dollar company. Like, come on, like, revving myself up because, like, I knew that if I couldn't succeed as running the company when we started, then like, the company would fail because we both needed to be doing equal amount and we need to be equally confident in our skills. And so I think it was like, I live life with a sense of urgency. And so I was like, I need to be this now, like yesterday, right? And so it's like immersing yourself in becoming that person. And it's funny because it only took me probably like a month and a half. And then like, two months later, I was, I was walking on the treadmill one day and I was like, I am this person now. So we got to take it away. We have a we have a sales manager, head coach, operations, and then a admin. I have, I don't give raises unless I give more responsibility. So like, that's what I've always used. Like if I give someone more responsibility, they deserve a raise. So like, it's, it's always like, if you're getting a raise, you're moving up to a higher level, or I'm delegating more responsibility to you, which frees me up to do things that are going to move the business forward faster, right? Now, I don't really believe in like raises for the sake of just giving someone a raise. Do you feel like you're working to our, do you feel like you're pushing to our, do we need, do you need help? And like on our weekly reports, always like, do you need support anywhere? And like really pushing for people to emphasize there and then like looking there at the end of the week or like, you know, and I do it with somebody, it's like, then you look at that and you're like, okay, how can I help you in the areas you need support so that we can reach this goal together? And so I think it's mostly giving opportunity for the person to let you know if they need help. That makes them feel okay. And then supporting them, like letting them know, like, you think they're awesome and appreciating them. Because then that gives someone confidence that even if they are being overworked and they feel like a little overwhelmed, they know that you believe in them enough that they can do it and reach that goal. Now, I'd say like, when I know that I'm pushing someone too far is when they start to get like snappy and angry, or negative, or like they kind of exclude themselves from the group and stop being as like interactive. And then I'm like, are you really stressed? And they're like, yeah. And then it's like, okay, let's look at what you're doing. A lot of times it's like, let's see where your time is going. Let's see if I can help you alleviate any of this by like having someone else help you that has more room on their plate, or having, you know, putting a different system in place or what it may be. But I would say 99% of times it's always solved with that conversation of like, where can I help?
